title: Renationalising British utilities - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Cowen argues against renationalising British utilities ahead of a PM change, noting state vs. private ownership shows little efficiency difference, but that turning utilities into 'AI companies' demands capital and pay scales the public sector can't manage.

insights.claim: US data indicate state-owned utilities are not meaningfully less efficient than privately-owned ones, especially water where natural-monopoly elements are strongest.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Undercuts the efficiency argument on both sides of the ownership debate — the case against renationalisation must rest elsewhere.
insights.quote: American data indicate that state-owned utilities do not seem very economically different than, or less efficient than, privately-owned utilities

insights.claim: Renationalising British utilities now would be a mistake because the necessary restructuring into 'AI companies' requires capital raises and pay scales the public sector cannot pull off.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Frames utility ownership through AI-era capital intensity — a signal that incumbents face large capex/talent demands regardless of who owns them.
insights.quote: That will require capital raises and pay scales that will be difficult for the public sector to pull off.
insights.locator: Nonetheless massive restructuring will be needed

insights.claim: The returns to resource mobility will be rising significantly going forward.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A macro claim that flexibility to reallocate capital and labor becomes more valuable — favors agile capital structures over rigid public ownership.
insights.quote: I would say the returns to resource mobility will be rising significantly.
